Docker is everywhere, and frontend devs are pretty likely to run into it too — so I figured I'd write a post about learning Docker.

Docker is an open-source project built in Go. It lets devs build, test, and deploy applications faster. If you've done cross-OS development, you've probably hit the problem where you need different packages and different versions on different OSes, which makes your dev environment inconsistent. Docker exists to solve exactly that.
The traditional approach is to spin up a VM, virtualise an OS, then install your packages inside. None of that's
per se — the painful part is tweaking the VM: network settings, disk space, etc. Docker lets you skip all that.
Before diving in, there are a few core concepts to know.
An "Image" in Docker isn't a picture — it's a Docker image, and it's read-only. If you've ever used a
file (e.g. with Alcohol 120%), an Image is basically that: a kind of disk image.
Alcohol 120% is a CD/DVD emulator that mounts
files on your computer as if you'd inserted a real disc.
If an Image is like a
disc image, a Container is what you get when you actually run it — like a CD drive with a disc inserted, which you can then run commands on. Those commands run inside the Container.
You can also think of a Container as a simple Linux environment you can do things in.
A Dockerfile is a plain text file with Docker commands in it. It can do a lot, but for now just know it's where you write commands.
Docker Hub is a Docker repository — it holds tons of Docker images you can download and use. Think of Docker Hub like GitHub, except GitHub holds source code and Docker Hub holds Docker images.
To use Docker you obviously need to install it. Just grab the installer from the
— the homepage detects your OS and gives you the right installer.
The install flow is straightforward so I won't screenshot every step.
If you see a version number, installation worked.
